summary:
The Naqada I culture, also called the Amratian culture after the site of El-Amra, represents an important phase in [[event:32|Predynastic Egyptian]] development, flourishing in Upper Egypt between approximately 4000 and 3500 BCE. Named after the large site of Naqada on the west bank of the Nile in the Qena governorate, this culture marks a significant step in the social and cultural development that would eventually lead to the unified Egyptian state.

Naqada I communities were agricultural, cultivating wheat and barley in the fertile Nile floodplain and herding cattle, sheep, goats, and pigs. Hunting and fishing supplemented the diet, as evidenced by the frequent depiction of wild animals — including hippopotami, crocodiles, and hunting scenes — in Naqada I art. Villages were typically located on elevated ground above the floodplain, positioned to avoid the annual Nile inundation while retaining easy access to agricultural land.

The most characteristic artifacts of the Naqada I culture are its distinctive white cross-lined pottery and red-polished black-topped ware. The white cross-lined vessels bear painted geometric designs, human figures, and animal motifs that offer a window into the symbolic and religious world of their makers. Figurines of women with upraised arms and animals, carved ivory combs and palettes for grinding cosmetics, and carved stone vessels indicate a growing investment in personal adornment and ritual practice. Flint-knapping reached a high level of refinement, producing elegant bifacially worked tools.

Burial practices during the Naqada I period show increasing elaboration and social differentiation. The dead were typically interred in oval or rectangular pit graves with the body in a contracted position, accompanied by pottery, stone tools, and personal ornaments. Wealthier individuals were buried with more numerous and finer goods, including imported materials such as turquoise from the Sinai and lapis lazuli, pointing to the existence of long-distance trade networks and the emergence of an elite class capable of accumulating luxury goods. These social distinctions foreshadow the pronounced hierarchy of the later Naqada phases and Pharaonic civilization.

The Naqada I culture was succeeded by the Naqada II (Gerzean) phase around 3500 BCE, which showed intensified trade connections with the Levant and Mesopotamia, expanded settlement sizes, and growing political consolidation — a trajectory that culminated in the unification of Egypt under the First Dynasty around 3100 BCE. The foundations of Egyptian artistic conventions, religious symbolism, and elite culture were firmly established during the Naqada sequence.

read_more_links.title: Egypt before the pharaohs - World History Encyclopedia
